name: Comparing Fractions — Which Is Bigger?
whatIsIt: Comparing fractions means finding which fraction is a bigger amount. The trick: a bigger BOTTOM number means SMALLER pieces! 1/8 is smaller than 1/2 because cutting into 8 parts makes tiny pieces. To compare fairly, we make the pieces the same size first — using equivalent fractions.
whyNeeded: Life is full of 'which is more?' questions with pieces: who got more cake, which bottle has more juice, which discount is bigger, which team finished more of the match. Without comparing fractions, Ravi will keep choosing 1/8 of the pizza thinking 8 means more!
realLifeUses: Food: is 3/4 of a chocolate bar more than 2/3 of the same bar?
Cricket: one team finished 3/4 of its overs, another 4/5 — who is further along?
Water bottles: 1/2 full vs 1/3 full — which has more water?
Homework: finishing 5/6 of your sums vs your friend's 7/8 — who is closer to done?

vocabulary.meaning: Decide which amount is bigger, smaller, or if they are equal.
The open mouth eats the BIGGER number: 3/4 > 1/4.
A friendly reference point, like 1/2, to judge other fractions against.

story.title: The Sly Jackal's Thin Little Slice
story.text: A sly jackal and a trusting deer found one big amla-and-jaggery poli. 'Let's be fair,' said the jackal slyly. 'YOU take one piece out of EIGHT — eight is such a big number! I'll take just one piece out of two.' The deer almost agreed — until Chatur the myna called from the tree: 'Careful, friend! The MORE cuts you make, the SMALLER each piece becomes. One out of eight is a thin little sliver; one out of two is half the whole poli. 1/2 is far more than 1/8!' The jackal slunk away, caught out.
story.extractedProblem: Which is more of the same poli: 1/2 or 1/8?
story.answerInStory: 1/2 is more. More cuts (a bigger bottom number) make smaller pieces, so 1/8 is a thin little slice.

visual.caption: Same pizza, one slice each. The bigger the bottom number, the smaller the slice: 1/2 > 1/4 > 1/8.

teachingGallery.title: Comparing fractions
teachingGallery.note: Same bottom? Bigger top wins. Same top? Smaller bottom wins (bigger slices).

standardMethod.summary: Make the bottoms the same (using equivalent fractions), then compare the tops.
steps: If bottoms are already the same: bigger top = bigger fraction (4/5 > 2/5).
If bottoms differ: rename one or both as equivalent fractions with the SAME bottom.
Example: compare 2/3 and 3/4 → rename as 8/12 and 9/12.
Now compare tops: 9 > 8, so 3/4 > 2/3.

alternateMethods.name: Same-top shortcut
alternateMethods.whenToUse: When the TOP numbers are the same, like 3/4 vs 3/8.
steps: Same number of pieces taken from each whole.
The fraction with the SMALLER bottom has BIGGER pieces.
So the smaller bottom wins: 3/4 > 3/8.

alternateMethods.name: Benchmark-half method
alternateMethods.whenToUse: For a quick estimate — is each fraction more or less than 1/2?
steps: Ask for each fraction: is the top more than half the bottom?
5/8: half of 8 is 4, and 5 > 4, so 5/8 is MORE than 1/2.
3/8: 3 < 4, so 3/8 is LESS than 1/2.
A more-than-half fraction beats a less-than-half fraction instantly.
alternateMethods.example: Compare 5/8 and 2/5: 5/8 > 1/2 and 2/5 < 1/2, so 5/8 > 2/5 — no renaming needed!

alternateMethods.name: Strip or number line check
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To SEE the comparison and verify your answer.
steps: Draw both fractions on equal strips (or one number line).
The shading that reaches further is the bigger fraction.
alternateMethods.example: Shade 2/3 and 3/4 on equal strips: 3/4 reaches further.

steps: The bottoms are the SAME (5) — the pieces are the same size.
Compare tops: 4 > 2.

visual.caption: Same-size parts — the longer bar wins: 4/5 is bigger than 2/5.

steps: Bottoms differ (3 and 4) — make them the same. A common bottom is 12.
2/3 = 8/12 (multiply top and bottom by 4).
3/4 = 9/12 (multiply top and bottom by 3).
Compare tops: 9 > 8, so 3/4 > 2/3.

visual.caption: Different cuts — 3/4 of the pizza is a little more than 2/3.

trickPractice.intro: Match one part: same bottom → biggest top; same top → smallest bottom.

hintLadder: Same BOTTOM? The bigger top wins. Same TOP? The smaller bottom wins.
Picture the slices.
3/5 is bigger.
Same BOTTOM? The bigger top wins. Same TOP? The smaller bottom wins.
Picture the slices.
1/3 is bigger.
Same BOTTOM? The bigger top wins. Same TOP? The smaller bottom wins.
Picture the slices.
3/4 is bigger.
Same BOTTOM? The bigger top wins. Same TOP? The smaller bottom wins.
Picture the slices.
1/2 is bigger.
Same BOTTOM? The bigger top wins. Same TOP? The smaller bottom wins.
Picture the slices.
7/8 is bigger.
Same BOTTOM? The bigger top wins. Same TOP? The smaller bottom wins.
Picture the slices.
2/3 is bigger.

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking a bigger bottom number means a bigger fraction (choosing 1/8 over 1/2 'because 8 > 2').
commonMistakes.fix: Remember Ravi's thin pizza slice! The bottom counts the CUTS — more cuts, smaller pieces. For the same top, the bigger bottom is the SMALLER fraction.

commonMistakes.mistake: Comparing only the top numbers when the bottoms are different (saying 3/8 > 2/3 because 3 > 2).
commonMistakes.fix: Tops can only be compared when the pieces are the same size. Make the bottoms equal first (equivalent fractions), THEN compare tops.

commonMistakes.mistake: Renaming wrongly while making bottoms equal (2/3 'equals' 3/4 by adding 1 to both).
commonMistakes.fix: Renaming uses MULTIPLY-both or DIVIDE-both only. Check your renamed fraction on strips before comparing.

easy.q: Same pizza. Which is MORE: 1/2 or 1/4?
hintLadder: Both take 1 piece — but from wholes cut differently.
Which cutting makes bigger pieces: 2 cuts or 4 cuts?
Fewer cuts = bigger pieces. 1 of 2 big pieces beats 1 of 4 smaller ones.
easy.explain: 1/2 > 1/4 — the bigger bottom made smaller pieces.

easy.q: Same bottoms! Which is bigger: 2/5 or 4/5?
hintLadder: The bottoms are the same, so all pieces are the same size.
Now just count the pieces: 2 pieces or 4 pieces?
More same-size pieces = bigger fraction.

medium.q: Same tops! Which is bigger: 3/4 or 3/8?
hintLadder: Both take 3 pieces — but which pieces are bigger?
Quarters or eighths — which cut makes bigger pieces?
3 big quarter-pieces beat 3 small eighth-pieces.

medium.q: Ravi says 3/8 is bigger than 2/3 'because 3 is more than 2.' What did he forget?

options.label: The pieces are different sizes — make the bottoms equal first
He should compare the bottoms only

medium.answer: The pieces are different sizes — make the bottoms equal first
hintLadder: Can you compare 3 tiny pieces with 2 big pieces just by counting?
Check with the half benchmark: is 3/8 more or less than 1/2? And 2/3?
3/8 < 1/2 but 2/3 > 1/2 — so 2/3 is bigger. Tops alone can lie!
Rename both with the same bottom. Try 12.
2/3 = 8/12 and 3/4 = 9/12 (multiply both parts).
Now compare tops: 9 > 8.

challenge.q: Quick benchmark thinking: which is bigger, 5/8 or 2/5?

options.label: 5/8, because it is more than 1/2 while 2/5 is less

challenge.answer: 5/8, because it is more than 1/2 while 2/5 is less
hintLadder: Compare each fraction with the friendly benchmark 1/2.
Half of 8 is 4 — is 5 more? Half of 5 is 2.5 — is 2 more?
5/8 is above half, 2/5 is below half. Above beats below!

questions.q: Same chocolate bar: which is MORE, 1/3 or 1/6?
hintLadder: One piece each — from 3 cuts or from 6 cuts.
Which cutting gives bigger pieces?
Fewer cuts, bigger piece: 1/3 > 1/6.
The bottoms match — same-size pieces.
Just count the pieces now.
5 pieces beat 3 pieces of the same size.
Rename 1/2 with 8 at the bottom.
1/2 = 4/8. Now compare with 3/8.
4 pieces beat 3 pieces of the same size.

questions.q: The one rule to remember before comparing tops:

options.label: The bottoms (piece sizes) must be the same first

questions.answer: The bottoms (piece sizes) must be the same first
hintLadder: Can you compare counts of different-sized pieces?
What did we use equivalent fractions for?
Equal bottoms = equal piece sizes = fair comparison.

teachBackPrompt: Tell the pizza story in YOUR words to someone at home: why did Ravi's choice of 1/8 go wrong, and what are the two ways to compare fractions fairly (same bottoms, or the 1/2 benchmark)?

revisionCard.summary: Bigger bottom = SMALLER pieces (1/8 < 1/2 — remember Ravi's thin slice!). Same bottoms → compare tops. Different bottoms → rename with equivalent fractions first, or use the 1/2 benchmark for a quick check.

gameMission.title: The Pizza Duel
gameMission.brief: Hungry guests keep choosing slices by the biggest bottom number! Rescue their dinners: pick the truly bigger fraction each round, using strips, renaming, or the half benchmark. Beat Ravi's 'big number' trick 8 times to win the Golden Slice.

realLifeProject: At dinner, cut one roti into halves and another into eighths. Ask a family member Ravi's question: '1/2 or 1/8 — which will you take?' Then show them why with the real pieces, and write down who fell for the trick!
